title: QA: env scrub uses denylist instead of allowlist (sensitive vars without matching suffix leak)
description:
## Severity
Major
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/tools/bash/policy.rs:143-151
## Violation
`apply_env_scrub` only removes env vars whose names match `is_sensitive_env` — a denylist. ANY sensitive credential whose name does not conform to the heuristics (e.g., `DB_URL`, `STRIPE_KEY` without `_API_KEY` suffix, `MONGODB_URI`, `DATABASE_URL`, `REDIS_URL`, `SLACK_WEBHOOK`, `JWT_PRIVATE_KEY_FILE`, or any custom name) flows freely into every spawned child.

Quoted (policy.rs:143-151):
```
pub fn apply_env_scrub(cmd: &mut Command) {
    let sensitive: Vec<String> = std::env::vars()
        .map(|(k, _)| k)
        .filter(|k| is_sensitive_env(k))
        .collect();
    for key in sensitive {
        cmd.env_remove(key);
    }
}
```

The comment ("Do NOT `env_clear`") acknowledges the tradeoff but ships the insecure default. OWASP A04 (Insecure Design).
## Mandated refactor
1. Flip to allowlist: `cmd.env_clear()` and then `cmd.env(key, val)` for each name in an explicit safe set (`PATH`, `HOME`, `USER`, `LANG`, `TERM`, `CARGO_HOME`, `RUST_LOG`, etc.).
2. Make the allowlist configurable via `.openclaudia/config.yaml` so operators can add project-specific safe vars.
3. Add a `passthrough_env: [...]` explicit opt-in for variables the model genuinely needs, audited per-deployment.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
